intro.title: Bike Anatomy
intro.subtitle: Every bicycle is a system of simple machines working together. Learn what each part does, and how few or how many parts a bike really needs.

minParts.why: Holds every other part together and carries the rider's weight.
Connects the front wheel to the frame and allows steering.
Rolls to reduce friction versus dragging on the ground.
Rolls and receives pedaling power through the chain.
Converts leg push into rotational force (torque).
The contact point where the rider's foot applies force.
Transfers pedaling power from the crankset to the rear wheel.
Supports the rider's body weight while pedaling.

maxFeatures.why: Lets the rider change gear ratio for hills or speed.
A set of different-sized gears for varying effort/speed.
Physically moves the chain between cassette gears.
Provides strong, consistent stopping power in all weather.
Absorbs bumps for comfort and control on rough terrain.
Improves visibility and safety at night.
Adds electric assistance for higher speed with less effort.
Stores electrical energy to power the motor.
Shows speed, battery, and ride data in real time.
Detects how hard the rider is pedaling to adjust motor assist.

systems.name: Frame & Steering System
systems.explanation: This system holds the bike together and lets the rider steer by turning the front wheel around the head tube axis.

systems.name: Wheel & Tyre System
systems.explanation: Wheels convert rolling motion into forward travel. Spokes hold tension, bearings reduce friction so wheels spin freely.

systems.name: Drivetrain (Power) System
systems.explanation: This system takes the rider's leg power and multiplies or reduces it using gears before sending it to the rear wheel.

systems.name: Braking System
systems.explanation: Braking converts the bike's motion (kinetic energy) into heat through friction, slowing the bike safely.

systems.name: Suspension System
systems.explanation: Springs and dampers absorb bumps from the terrain, keeping wheels in contact with the ground for control and comfort.

systems.name: Comfort & Accessories
systems.explanation: These parts don't move the bike, but they make riding safer, more comfortable, and more convenient.

systems.name: Electric Power System
systems.explanation: Sensors detect rider effort, the controller decides how much power to send, and the motor adds assistance from the battery.

1.title: Level 1 — Just Movable
1.description: The absolute minimum parts needed so the bicycle can roll and be pedaled. Great for understanding the core mechanics.
1.goal: Build a bike that moves — nothing fancy, just working mechanics.

2.title: Level 2 — Maximum Needs & Features
2.description: A fully equipped bicycle with gearing, braking systems, and comfort accessories like lights, mudguards, and bottle holders.
2.goal: Build a complete, real-world-ready bicycle with every standard feature.

3.title: Level 3 — Advanced & Inventive
3.description: Push beyond standard design. Add suspension, sensors, electric assistance, or entirely new invented features.
3.goal: Design an adventurous, high-performance, or futuristic bicycle.
